coredata.dc:title: Integration of Eu-based metal-organic frameworks and carbon dots for multicolor visual intelligent detection of phosphate
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yongxin
Zhou, Rongrong
Zhang, Lina
Bi, Ning
Gou, Jian
Wu, Jiang
Jia, Lei
Xu, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Phosphate (Pi) has an important influence on the water environment and physiological processes. Therefore, developing fluorescent probe for quantitative detection of Pi is crucial for water environment monitoring and human health assessment. This work designed a dual-emission ratio nano-fluorescent probe GCDs/Eu-BDC based on europium-based metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs) and blue carbon dots (GCDs) for multicolor fluorescence detection of Pi. The GCDs/Eu-BDC realized multicolor fluorescence detection of Pi based on the red-to-blue fluorescence change. The probe has high selectivity and a detection limit of 70 nM in the range of 0–45 μM. GCDs/Eu-BDC can be used to detect Pi in environmental water samples and serum samples, proving the feasibility of quantitative analysis of Pi in real samples. In addition, a portable paper-based sensor was prepared in this work. Combined with the chromaticity analysis App in smartphones, the intelligent real-time detection of Pi can be realized, which has certain practical application potential.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphate
Ratiometric fluorescence
Nanoprobes
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GCDs and Eu-BDC MOFs
The detection of Pi by GCDs/Eu-BDC in real samples
Portable test paper for visual detection of Pi
Microstructure characterization of GCDs and Eu-BDC
The fluorescence sensing mechanism of GCDs/Eu-BDC
Visual detection of Pi by functional test paper
Detection of Pi in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Youth Program of Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (232300420300), Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province (242102230076), Key Scientific Research Projects of Universities in Henan Province (25A430015), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan (222300420047, 242300421247), the Foundation of the Science and Technology Innovation Team in Henan Universities (24IRTSTHN014), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51773052, U1904168), the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University (T2021-2), and the Doctoral Fund for Henan Polytechnic University (B2022-46).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of GCDs. The insert is particle size distribution. (b) SEM image of Eu-BDC. (c) FT-IR spectra of GCDs and Eu-BDC. (d) XRD patterns of Eu-BDC.
(a) The full scan XPS spectrum of GCDs. (b) The full scan XPS spectra of Eu-BDC. (c, d, e) High-resolution XPS spectra of the GCDs. (f) High-resolution XPS spectra of Eu 3d.

(a) Fluorescence decay (λem = 617 nm) curves of GCDs/Eu-BDC ratiometric fluorescent probe after adding Pi. (b) The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of Pi and the emission spectrum of Eu-BDC. (c) The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of Pi and the excitation spectrum of Eu-BDC. (d) The XRD patterns of Eu-BDC after the addition of Pi. (e) FT-IR spectra of Eu-BDC and Eu-BDC-Pi. (f) The full scan XPS spectrum of Eu-BDC-Pi.

(a) Response of ratio fluorescent probe to Pi. (b) The piecewise fitting linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (I617/I472) and Pi concentration (0–10 μM and 10–45 μM). (c) CIE chromaticity diagram in the presence of different concentrations of Pi. (d) The fluorescence image under a UV lamp of 254 nm.

(a) The effect of interference on I617/I472 (the concentration of Pi and interferer were fixed at 40 μM). (b) Fluorescence photograph of GCDs/Eu-BDC nanoprobe with interfering substances (Ba2+, Na+, Mn2+, K+, HSO3 −, Br−, Cl−, SO4 2−, S2−, I−, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, AC−, HAsO4 2−) under 254 nm UV lamp. (c) Ultraviolet fluorescence photos of GCDs/Eu-BDC nanoprobe in response to Pi in the presence of interfering ions.

The fluorescence color images of GCDs/Eu-BDC test paper under different concentrations of Pi and 254 nm ultraviolet lamp, the function diagram between red (R) and blue (B) ratio to Pi concentration obtained by chromaticity analysis.
Preparation of GCDs/Eu-BDC and multicolor fluorescence detection of Pi.

head.ce:title: Integration of Eu-based metal-organic frameworks and carbon dots for multicolor visual intelligent detection of phosphate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Qinghai Nationalities University, Xining 810007, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Qinghai Nationalities University, Xining 810007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosphate (Pi) has an important influence on the water environment and physiological processes. Therefore, developing fluorescent probe for quantitative detection of Pi is crucial for water environment monitoring and human health assessment. This work designed a dual-emission ratio nano-fluorescent probe GCDs/Eu-BDC based on europium-based metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs) and blue carbon dots (GCDs) for multicolor fluorescence detection of Pi. The GCDs/Eu-BDC realized multicolor fluorescence detection of Pi based on the red-to-blue fluorescence change. The probe has high selectivity and a detection limit of 70 nM in the range of 0–45 μM. GCDs/Eu-BDC can be used to detect Pi in environmental water samples and serum samples, proving the feasibility of quantitative analysis of Pi in real samples. In addition, a portable paper-based sensor was prepared in this work. Combined with the chromaticity analysis App in smartphones, the intelligent real-time detection of Pi can be realized, which has certain practical application potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphate (Pi) is an important anion that plays an important role in the water environment and physiological processes [1,2]. In the water environment, Pi is an important nutrient that stimulates the growth of aquatic plants [3]. However, excessive Pi (0.2 mg/L) can lead to serious environmental problems such as eutrophication of natural water bodies and water quality decline. Therefore, Pi can be used as an indicator to measure the quality of water bodies to a certain extent [4,5]. In addition, Pi is one of the most important anions in biological systems. It is involved in physiological processes such as cell signal transduction, cell membrane composition, and energy metabolism. Abnormal Pi concentration in the human body can lead to hypophosphatemia and hyperphosphatemia, which further leads to a series of diseases such as rickets, hyperthyroidism, and cardiovascular complications [6–9]. Therefore, developing an effective Pi detection method is significant for water quality monitoring and clinical diagnosis.

At present, a variety of methods for Pi detection have been established, such as the colorimetric method [10–13], electrochemical method [14–17], and spectrophotometry [18,19]. The fluorescence method has been widely used because of its high sensitivity, simple operation, good selectivity, fast response, and low cost [20–22]. Compared with the fluorescence change of a single response signal, the ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe can eliminate the influence of background interference and become one of the most promising methods for quantitatively detecting analytes [23]. However, the establishment of a simple ratiometric fluorescence system with good performance is a challenging task.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous crystalline materials formed by coordinating metal ions and organic ligands. It has the advantages of a large specific surface area and adjustable pore size and is widely used in biosensors [24,25]. Among them, lanthanide metal-organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) exhibit unique luminescent properties, including narrow emission band, long fluorescence lifetime, large Stokes shift, and good fluorescence monochromaticity. Therefore, it is a high-quality material for constructing fluorescent probes [26–30]. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been applied in biosensing, bioimaging, ion detection, and catalytic materials due to their low-cost green synthesis, high fluorescence quantum yield, good biocompatibility, and photostability [31–35]. The surface of carbon dots generally contains a large number of easily modified functional groups (-COOH, –OH, –NH2), which can be combined with Ln-MOFs materials to construct ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes [36–40].

In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe was constructed by combining blue carbon dots (GCDs) and Eu-MOFs (Eu-BDC) for visual fluorescence detection of Pi. As shown in Scheme 1 , GCDs contain many carboxyl groups mixed with Eu-BDC and aggregated on their surface. The blue fluorescence of GCDs is quenched by their aggregation, and the ratiometric fluorescent probe shows the red fluorescence of Eu-BDC. After the addition of Pi, due to the high affinity between Pi and Eu3+, the combination of Pi and Eu3+ destroyed the structure of Eu-BDC and interrupted the antenna effect process, resulting in the red fluorescence quenching of Eu-BDC. At the same time, the aggregation state of GCDs was destroyed, and their blue fluorescence was restored. Based on the fluorescence change from red to blue, the visual detection of Pi is realized. In addition, with the help of test paper and color analysis APP on a smartphone, real-time intelligent detection of Pi can be realized, which has excellent potential in on-site analysis and detection of Pi.

Disodium terephthalate (Na2BDC, Sodium Terephthalate) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Co., Ltd. 3,4,9,10-Perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) and europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O) were purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Triethylamine (TEA) and Trisodium phosphate dodecahydrate (Na3PO4·12H2O) (98 %) were purchased from Tianjin Weichen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The reagents used in the experiment were analytical reagents and can be used without further purification.

The material's microstructure was observed by JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, 200 kV accelerating voltage). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were taken using Merlin Compact scanning electron microscope (Carl Zeiss NTS GmbH). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern data (2θ = 5°–50°) were measured by the Rigaku Dmax 2400 diffractometer. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, 4000-500 cm−1) of the material was measured by mixing the solid powder with the KBr particles and pressing them into a transparent sheet by a Bruker V70 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The surface composition of the samples was analyzed by Thermo SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed using the Edinburgh FLS 1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence phosphorescence spectrometer. The steady-state emission spectra were obtained by the F97 Pro fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GCDs and Eu-BDC MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: GCDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method [41]. In short, 0.52 g PTCDA and 2 mL TEA were completely dissolved in 8 mL ultrapure water. The above mixed solution was transferred to a 20 mL reaction kettle, placed in an oven at 220 °C for 48 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The supernatant after centrifugation (9700 rpm/min) was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and placed in a cellulose membrane (MWCO 500), and dialyzed with deionized water for 30 h. The powdered solid was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying. Eu-BDC MOFs were synthesized at room temperature [42]. 0.0668 g Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and 0.0314 g Na2BDC were dissolved in 40 mL deionized water. After stirring at room temperature for 30 min, the white precipitate was obtained by centrifugation at 9700 rpm for 3 min. Eu-BDC powder was obtained after washing with deionized water and drying at 60 °C.

Eu-BDC aqueous solution (1 mg/mL, 70 μL) and GCDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg/mL, 10 μL) were placed in a quartz cuvette. The pH = 7.4 Tris-HCl buffer solution was added to make the total volume of the solution reach 2 mL to construct a fluorescence detection system. Different concentrations of Pi were added to the above system and incubated at room temperature for 4 min. The fluorescence emission spectrum (λex = 280 nm) was recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer to study the detection effect of the nanoprobe on Pi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of Pi by GCDs/Eu-BDC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Lake water, tap water, and fetal bovine serum were used as actual samples. The lake's water is from the Xinyue Lake of Henan Polytechnic University. After centrifugation of the lake water for 5 min (9700 rpm), the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane. Tap water is taken from the laboratory without additional treatment. Fetal bovine serum was purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd., and 0.5 mL of serum was mixed with 2.0 mL of acetonitrile to remove protein. After centrifugation of the mixed solution for 15 min (12000 rpm), the supernatant was transferred to a 25 mL volumetric flask and diluted with ultrapure water to a scale for use [43]. The actual samples were added to the nanoprobe. The Pi standard solution was added to the above system, and the fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded. The concentration and recovery of Pi in the sample were calculated by fitting the curve. Each sample was measured three times, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable test paper for visual detection of Pi
ce:section.ce:para: The portable test paper was prepared by using filter paper. The filter paper was cut into circles and dispersed in GCDs/Eu-BDC solution for 1 h. After soaking, the filter paper was placed on a glass slide and dried naturally. Different concentrations of Pi solution were dropped on the prepared test paper, and the fluorescence color change of the test paper was observed under the irradiation of a 254 nm ultraviolet lamp. Through the color scanning of the mobile phone color scanning App, the relationship between Red/Blue (R/B) and Pi concentration is analyzed, and the visual portable detection of Pi can be realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure characterization of GCDs and Eu-BDC
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of GCDs was observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, GCDs have good dispersibility. The particle size distribution is between 0.5 and 3.5 nm. According to the UV–visible absorption spectra of GCDs (Fig. S1), 254 nm is a typical absorption band caused by the π-π∗ transition of CC, and there are also absorption bands of 418 nm and 443 nm caused by the n-π∗ transition of surface groups [41,44]. The surface groups and structures of GCDs were studied by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1c, the strong absorption peak near 3435 cm−1 originates from the stretching vibration of N–H/O–H [45]. The absorption peak near 1636 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CO. The absorption peak near 1400 cm−1 is derived from the stretching vibration of C–N [44,46,47]. The absorption peak at 818 cm−1 is attributed to the planar bending of the benzene ring [41]. According to the XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 a), GCDs contain C, N and O elements. From the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra (Fig. 2c–e), GCDs contain C–C, C–O, C–N, N–H, and CO bonds [48]. The above results prove that GCDs are successfully prepared.

The morphology of Eu-BDC was observed by SEM as a flower-like sphere composed of a flake structure (Fig. 1b). The sheet structure exhibited a triclinic structure by TEM (Fig. S2). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Eu-BDC (Fig. 1d) confirmed its crystal structure [49]. The surface groups and structure of Eu-BDC were characterized by FT-IR, as shown in Fig. 1c. The strong absorption peak at 3454 cm−1 is derived from the O–H stretching vibration of water molecules. This indicates that water acts as a solvent and participates in the reaction process [50,51]. In addition, the characteristic peaks of Eu-BDC at 1538 cm−1 and 1404 cm−1 are based on carboxyl groups' asymmetric and symmetric vibrations [52]. The elemental composition of Eu-BDC was analyzed by XPS spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2b, the diffraction peaks at 1134.6 eV, 532.6 eV, and 284.9 eV are attributed to Eu 3d, O 1s, and C 1s, respectively. Among them, the high resolution energy spectra of O1s and C1s are shown in Fig. S3. There are four characteristic peaks caused by the spin-orbit interaction of Eu in the high-resolution energy spectrum of Eu 3d (Fig. 2f). The binding energy potentials are 1164.5 eV, 1155.3 eV, 1134.8 eV, and 1124.5 eV, which correspond to the energy spectrum peaks of Eu3+ 3d3/2, Eu2+ 3d3/2, Eu3+ 3d5/2, and Eu2+ 3d5/2, respectively [53]. The above results confirmed the successful synthesis of Eu-BDC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence sensing mechanism of GCDs/Eu-BDC
ce:section.ce:para: The following studies were carried out to clarify the detection mechanism of GCDs/Eu-BDC for Pi. First, the fluorescence emission spectrum did not change before and after adding Pi to the GCDs solution (Fig. S4). After mixing GCDs with Eu-BDC solution, the blue fluorescence of GCDs is greatly weakened. With the addition of Pi, its fluorescence gradually recovered. This is attributed to the fact that the surface of GCDs contains many carboxyl groups and amino groups, and the carboxyl groups on the surface of GCDs are coordinated with Eu3+ after mixing with Eu-BDC. GCDs aggregate on the surface of Eu-BDC, resulting in fluorescence quenching of GCDs (Figs. S5a and b). The affinity of Eu3+ to the oxygen donor atom in Pi is higher than that of the carboxyl group on the surface of GCDs, so the aggregation state of GCDs is destroyed after the addition of Pi, and the blue fluorescence of GCDs is restored (Figs. S5c–f) [41].

Secondly, the strong interaction between Eu-BDC and Pi leads to the red fluorescence quenching of Eu-BDC at 612 nm. The quenching mechanism of Eu-BDC luminescence may include fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), internal filter effect (IFE), and disintegration of the skeleton structure [54]. Firstly, the fluorescence lifetime of GCDs/Eu-BDC with 40 μM Pi was measured. As shown in Fig. 3 a, compared with the fluorescence lifetime of GCDs/Eu-BDC without Pi addition, there was no significant change, indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was not FRET and PET. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Pi and the emission spectra of Eu-BDC were compared (Fig. 3b). There is no overlap between the two spectra, which further confirms that the quenching mechanism is not FRET. Comparing the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Pi with the excitation spectrum of Eu-BDC, Fig. 3c shows no overlap between the two, so the IFE mechanism can be excluded [55].

Finally, the morphology of Eu-BDC after Pi treatment was observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. S6, compared with Eu-BDC (Fig. 1b), the surface of Eu-BDC-Pi becomes rough, and obvious fragments can be observed. This indicates that Pi may coordinate with Eu3+ and destroy the structure of Eu-BDC. In addition, compared with the X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of Eu-BDC before and after Pi treatment (Fig. 3d), the results showed that the diffraction peak intensity of the XRD pattern of Eu-BDC-Pi was significantly reduced. This indicates that the original skeleton and corresponding structure of Eu-BDC are destroyed. Subsequently, the FT-IR spectra of Eu-BDC-Pi were measured. As shown in Fig. 3e, a new characteristic peak appears at 1070 cm−1, which is derived from the stretching vibration of P–O. The peak of P 2p can also be observed at 133 ev in the XPS spectrum of Eu-BDC-Pi (Fig. 3f), indicating that Pi was successfully embedded in MOF. After adding Pi, the binding energies of Eu3+ 3d3/2 and Eu3+ 3d5/2 in the XPS spectra of Eu 3d were 1164.5 eV and 1134.8 eV, respectively, which moved to the high energy side (Fig. S7). This phenomenon indicates that the P–O-Eu coordination bond replaces the coordination between Eu3+ and carboxylate. Because the electrons inside the atom are shielded by both the nuclear charge and the Coulomb force of the extranuclear electrons, when Pi is combined with Eu3+, the outer electron density of Eu3+ decreases, and the shielding effect is weakened, which will cause it to move in the direction of high binding energy [56]. In summary, it is reasonable to speculate that the quenching mechanism of Eu-BDC red fluorescence is the collapse of Eu-BDC MOFs structure caused by the strong interaction between Pi and Eu3+ after the introduction of Pi.

The best detection conditions were obtained through the following research. Firstly, the optimal excitation wavelength of the ratiometric fluorescent probe was determined. The optimal excitation wavelength of GCDs is 255 nm (Fig. S8a). The optimum excitation wavelength of Eu-BDC is 280 nm, and it shows a strong fluorescence emission peak at 617 nm (Fig. S8b). This is because Eu3+ is sensitized by the ligand, which transitions from the ground state (5D0) to the excited state (7F2), resulting in strong fluorescence emission of Eu3+ at 617 nm. To make the change of Eu-BDC fluorescence signal more obvious, 280 nm was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength of the ratio fluorescent probe (Fig. S9). Secondly, the response of GCDs/Eu-BDC to Pi at different pH values was discussed. GCDs/Eu-BDC was used to detect Pi in buffer solutions with different pH values. The fluorescence intensity ratio I617/I472 of the probe before and after adding Pi reached the maximum at pH = 7.4 (Fig. S10). For the optimal detection time, I617/I472 remained basically stable after 4 min after adding 30 μM Pi (Fig. S11), so 4 min was selected as the optimal response time. In addition, the stability of GCDs/Eu-BDC under high-pressure xenon lamp illumination for 800 s was also studied. Before and after adding Pi, the fluorescence intensity of GCDs/Eu-BDC at 472 nm and 617 nm did not change significantly within 800 s (Fig. S12), indicating that the ratiometric fluorescent probe had good light stability.

Under the optimal detection conditions, the sensitivity of GCDs/Eu-BDC to Pi was studied by the fluorescence response after adding different concentrations of Pi. According to the fluorescence emission spectra measured after adding different concentrations of Pi (Fig. 4 a), with the increase of Pi concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 472 nm gradually increased, and the fluorescence intensity at 593 nm, 617 nm, 651 nm, and 699 nm decreased significantly. As shown in Fig. 4b, the I617/I472 value showed a good linear relationship with Pi concentration in the range of 0–10 μM (R2 = 0.99816) and 10–45 μM (R2 = 0.98239). According to the detection limit calculation formula LOD = 3σ/slope (σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample). The LOD is 70 nM, which is lower than the detection requirement of phosphate wastewater standard in a water environment (6.4–320 μM) [57,58]. In addition, with the increase of Pi concentration, GCDs/Eu-BDC showed obvious color change under a 254 nm UV lamp (Fig. 4d). The CIE chromaticity coordinates were calculated by the fluorescence spectra corresponding to different concentrations of Pi. As shown in Fig. 4c and Table S1, the CIE coordinates changed from red (0.4885, 0.3219) to blue (0.2980, 0.2903) as the concentration of Pi increased from 0 μM to 45 μM. The above results provide a basis for the visual detection of Pi.

Considering the complexity of the actual sample, the selectivity of GCDs/Eu-BDC to Pi and its anti-interference were further studied. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity ratio I617/I472 did not change significantly after adding other metal ions and anions, indicating that the fluorescent probe had a specific response to Pi. At the same time, as shown in Fig. 5b, only Pi can cause a significant change in the fluorescence color of GCDs/Eu-BDC under a 254 nm UV lamp, which provides the possibility for visual qualitative detection of Pi. In addition, Fig. 5c also shows that the fluorescent probe can be used to detect Pi in the presence of interfering ions, indicating that the fluorescent probe has good anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of Pi by functional test paper
ce:section.ce:para: A portable paper sensor was prepared by immobilizing the GCDs/Eu-BDC fluorescent probe on filter paper. As shown in Fig. 6 , the filter paper sensor exhibits red fluorescence under 254 nm UV light irradiation. The fluorescence gradually changed from red to blue with the increase of Pi concentration. Then, the color scanning App on the mobile phone was used to collect the color of the fluorescent filter paper after dropping different concentrations of Pi under 254 nm UV light. Convert the fluorescence color to RGB values [59]. The results showed that the concentration of Pi and its corresponding ratio of blue to red (B/R) showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0–45 μM, which can be used to realize the visual qualitative and semi-quantitative detection of Pi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pi in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of GCDs/Eu-BDC ratiometric fluorescent probe in practical application, the detection results of Pi in tap water, lake water and serum were studied by standard addition method. The initial concentration of Pi in real samples determined by ion chromatography is extremely low. Different concentrations of Pi were added to the actual sample to test the fluorescence emission spectrum. The recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated. According to Table S2, the measured recovery was in the range of 91.91–103.65 %. The results show that GCDs/Eu-BDC has certain practical application in the detection of Pi. In addition, this method is compared with the reported Pi detection method (Table S3). The multicolor nano-fluorescent probes in this work have certain advantages in terms of detection limits, test papers, and on-site intelligent detection.

In summary, this study developed a multicolor fluorescent nanoprobe GCDs/Eu-BDC based on carbon dots and MOFs, which can realize the visual quantitative detection of Pi. The probe has good anti-interference ability, high selectivity, and high sensitivity, and the detection limit is 70 nM. The transition of the fluorescence sensing signal from red to blue can realize the visual detection of Pi. The nano-fluorescent probe also applies to the detection of Pi in samples such as lake water and serum and has broad application prospects. In addition, based on the multicolor fluorescence change from red to blue of GCDs/Eu-BDC to Pi, a portable paper-based sensor was developed. By analyzing and processing smartphone colorimetric analysis App data, the correlation between Pi concentration and B/R value was obtained, which provided the possibility for on-site analysis of Pi in actual samples.

Yongxin Li: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Rongrong Zhou: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lina Zhang: Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ning Bi: Visualization, Validation, Methodology. Jian Gou: Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Jiang Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Resources. Lei Jia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jun Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Youth Program of Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (232300420300), Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province (242102230076), Key Scientific Research Projects of Universities in Henan Province (25A430015), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan (222300420047, 242300421247), the Foundation of the Science and Technology Innovation Team in Henan Universities (24IRTSTHN014), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51773052, U1904168), the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University (T2021-2), and the Doctoral Fund for Henan Polytechnic University (B2022-46).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.127270.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design principles and applications of selective lanthanide-based receptors for inorganic phosphate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. C. Pierre, R. K. Wilharm, Design principles and applications of selective lanthanide-based receptors for inorganic phosphate, Frontiers in Chemistry, 10 (2022) 821020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and applications of metal-based molecular receptors and probes for inorganic phosphate

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence-scattering dual-signal response of carbon dots@ZIF-90 for phosphate ratiometric detection
